title: Fiscal, monetary & supply-side policy
summary:
## Three families of macro policy

Governments have three main policy toolkits. **Fiscal** and **monetary** policy are **demand-side** (they shift AD); **supply-side** policy shifts **LRAS**.

## 1. Fiscal policy

**Fiscal policy** = the use of **government spending (G)** and **taxation (T)** to influence AD and the economy.
- **Expansionary** — cut taxes and/or raise spending → **AD rises** (used to fight recession/unemployment). Usually widens the **budget deficit**.
- **Contractionary** — raise taxes and/or cut spending → **AD falls** (used to curb inflation).
- **Direct/indirect taxes**; a **budget deficit** (G > T) adds to **national debt**, a **surplus** (T > G) reduces it.
- **Strengths:** can target specific groups/regions; government spending directly creates demand; can also improve supply (infrastructure, education).
- **Weaknesses:** time lags, risk of rising debt and interest costs, **crowding out** of private investment, political constraints.

## 2. Monetary policy

**Monetary policy** = a central bank's use of **interest rates**, the **money supply** and (later) **quantitative easing** to influence AD, usually to hit an inflation target.
- **Lower interest rates** → cheaper borrowing, less saving, higher C and I, weaker currency (↑X−M) → **AD rises**.
- **Higher interest rates** → the reverse, cooling demand and inflation.
- **Strengths:** flexible, run by an independent central bank (credibility), affects the whole economy.
- **Weaknesses:** time lags (~18 months), ineffective at very low rates (liquidity trap), depends on confidence, and hits borrowers/exporters unevenly.

## 3. Supply-side policy

**Supply-side policies** aim to raise the economy's **productive capacity and efficiency**, shifting **LRAS right** (potential growth) — the only way to raise growth *without* inflation in the long run.
- **Market-based:** cut income/corporation tax to sharpen incentives, deregulation, privatisation, trade-union reform, reducing benefits to boost work incentives.
- **Interventionist:** government spending on **education & training, infrastructure, healthcare, R&D** to raise productivity and human capital.
- **Strengths:** can improve *several* objectives at once (growth, jobs, lower inflation, better trade balance).
- **Weaknesses:** **long time lags**, costly, uncertain success, and some measures raise **inequality**.

## Choosing and combining policies

The right mix depends on the **cause** of the problem: demand-side tools for cyclical/demand shocks; supply-side tools for structural weakness and long-run growth. Policies are often **combined** (e.g. loose monetary + supply-side reform).

## Why it matters

This chapter is the payoff of AS macro: it applies the AD/AS model to real policy and is the heart of ‘evaluate the best policy to achieve X’ essays in both AS and A2 (Unit 10).

keyTerms.definition: Using government spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand.
When government spending exceeds tax revenue in a period; adds to national debt.
A central bank's use of interest rates and the money supply to influence AD.
The price/reward of borrowing and saving money; the main monetary-policy tool.
Policies to raise productive capacity and efficiency, shifting LRAS right.
When higher government borrowing/spending reduces private-sector investment.
Demand-side policy shifts AD; supply-side policy shifts LRAS.

diagrams: **Expansionary demand-side policy:** AD shifts right on AD/AS → higher output (and possibly higher price level near capacity).
**Contractionary policy:** AD shifts left → lower price level and output.
**Supply-side policy:** LRAS (and SRAS) shift right → higher output with a *lower* price level — the ideal outcome.
tips: Classify tools correctly: **fiscal & monetary = demand-side (shift AD)**; **supply-side = shift LRAS**.
For every policy, give **strengths AND weaknesses** (time lags, debt, crowding out, uncertainty) to reach the top bands.
Match the **policy to the cause**: demand-side for cyclical problems, supply-side for structural/long-run growth.
Stress that only **supply-side/LRAS growth** raises output *without* inflation in the long run — a key evaluation line.
Note the **conflicts/side-effects** (e.g. supply-side reforms raising inequality; higher rates hurting exporters).

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Comprehensive fiscal, monetary and supply-side coverage.
